audit.methodology: OWASP Top 10 2021 + Manual Code Review + Automated Scanning
audit.scope: Full application source code, configuration, infrastructure, and dependencies

findings.title: Hardcoded Superadmin Credentials in Database Seed
findings.description: The database seed file contains a hardcoded superadmin user (superadmin@openapi-sri.com) with a known bcrypt password hash ($2b$12$85teQgrnCqABaMn.DH0b3O8.M3Zk5RhUuZe3J/rqsgBlDqCSVFRKm). If deployed without changing credentials, an attacker can crack the bcrypt hash and gain full SUPERADMIN access to the entire multi-tenant system.
findings.impact: Full system compromise. Attacker gains SUPERADMIN access to all tenants, all emisores, all certificates, all financial documents, and can issue arbitrary electronic invoices.
findings.evidence: database/init.sql line 1015: INSERT INTO public.usuarios ... VALUES ('00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000', 'superadmin@openapi-sri.com', '$2b$12$85teQgrnCqABaMn.DH0b3O8.M3Zk5RhUuZe3J/rqsgBlDqCSVFRKm', 'SUPERADMIN', NULL, true, ...)
findings.remediation: 1. Remove the hardcoded seed user from init.sql. 2. Generate a unique superadmin password at deployment time. 3. Force password change on first login. 4. Add a deployment checklist that requires changing default credentials.

findings.title: Certificate Password Stored in Cleartext Column
findings.description: The emisores table has a certificado_password (text) column that stores the P12 certificate password in cleartext, alongside the encrypted certificado_password_encrypted column. Any database read (backup, SQL dump, compromised query) exposes all P12 certificate passwords in plaintext.
findings.impact: Exposure of all P12 certificate private key passwords. An attacker with database read access can decrypt all electronic signing certificates and issue fraudulent invoices on behalf of any tenant.
findings.evidence: database/init.sql line 444: certificado_password text — alongside line 450: certificado_password_encrypted text. The emisores.service.ts uploadCertificado() method stores the encrypted version, but the old column remains.
findings.remediation: 1. Migrate all existing certificado_password values to certificado_password_encrypted using EncryptionService. 2. Drop the certificado_password column. 3. Update all code that references the old column. 4. Run a database migration to remove the plaintext column.

findings.title: Path Traversal in Certificate Service File Operations
findings.description: CertificateService.certificateExists(), deleteCertificate(), and getCertificatePath() use join(this.certsDir, fileName) without validating that the resolved path stays within the certificates directory. While the controller checks for .p12 extension, it does not prevent path traversal sequences like '../../etc/passwd.p12'. The xml-signer.service.ts has proper protection (line 74-79), but the certificate service does not.
findings.impact: An authenticated attacker could delete or enumerate arbitrary .p12 files on the server filesystem. Combined with file upload, could overwrite critical files.
findings.evidence: certificate.service.ts line 87-88: certificateExists(fileName) { const filePath = join(this.certsDir, fileName); return existsSync(filePath); } — No path.resolve() or startsWith() check. compare with xml-signer.service.ts line 74-79 which has proper protection.
findings.remediation: 1. Add path.resolve() and startsWith(certsBaseDir) validation to all file operations in CertificateService, matching the pattern used in XmlSignerService. 2. Reject filenames containing '..' or '/'.

findings.title: Webhook Secret Exposed in API Responses
findings.description: All webhook CRUD operations (findAll, findOne, create, update, delete, regenerateSecret) return the secreto field in the response. The webhook secret is used to sign HMAC payloads for delivery verification. Exposing it in API responses allows any authenticated user to forge webhook payloads.
findings.impact: An attacker can forge webhook payloads that appear legitimate, potentially triggering false business events or exploiting downstream systems that trust webhook signatures.
findings.evidence: webhooks.service.ts lines 63, 87, 106, 125, 179, 198: SQL queries SELECT and RETURN the 'secreto' column. mapToResponse() at line 339: secreto: row.secreto as string.
findings.remediation: 1. Exclude 'secreto' from all SELECT queries in read operations. 2. Create a WebhookSecretResponseDto that omits the secret. 3. Only return the secret once during creation via a separate secure endpoint. 4. Add regenerateSecret() to return the new secret only in the response, not store it in list queries.

findings.title: Missing Tenant Isolation on Emisores findAll()
findings.description: EmisoresService.findAll() returns ALL emisores across ALL tenants without any tenant filtering. While findAllByTenant() exists, if any controller route uses findAll() instead of findAllByTenant(), it leaks cross-tenant data. The SRI controllers or admin endpoints might use this unprotected method.
findings.impact: Cross-tenant data leakage. A user from Tenant A can see all emisores (companies, RUCs, certificate status) from Tenant B, violating multi-tenant isolation.
findings.evidence: emisores.service.ts line 48-60: findAll() { const result = await this.db.query('SELECT ... FROM emisores ORDER BY created_at DESC'); } — No WHERE tenant_id = clause.
findings.remediation: 1. Remove or restrict findAll() to SUPERADMIN only. 2. Audit all controller routes to ensure they use findAllByTenant() or validateEmisorAccess(). 3. Add a @Roles(UserRole.SUPERADMIN) guard on any endpoint that uses findAll().

findings.title: No Account Lockout After Failed Login Attempts
findings.description: The login endpoint has rate limiting (5 attempts per minute per IP via @Throttle), but there is no account-level lockout mechanism. An attacker can distribute login attempts across multiple IPs or rotate IPs to bypass the per-IP rate limit and brute-force passwords indefinitely.
findings.impact: Credential brute-force attacks become feasible with distributed IP rotation. Combined with weak passwords, this can lead to account compromise.
findings.evidence: auth.controller.ts line 45: @Throttle({ default: { limit: 5, ttl: 60000 } }) — IP-based only, no account lockout. auth.service.ts: no failed_attempt counter or lock logic.
findings.remediation: 1. Implement account-level lockout after 5 failed attempts (e.g., lock for 15 minutes). 2. Track failed attempts in the usuarios table (failed_attempts, locked_until columns). 3. Add exponential backoff for repeated failures.

findings.title: CORS Allows Null Origin Requests
findings.description: The CORS configuration allows requests with no Origin header (null origin) to bypass all origin restrictions. This is documented as allowing 'Postman, curl, mobile apps' but in production it enables CSRF-like attacks from malicious pages using sandboxed iframes.
findings.impact: A malicious website can make cross-origin requests to the API from a sandboxed iframe (which sends no Origin), bypassing CORS protections entirely.
findings.evidence: main.ts line 43-44: origin: (origin, callback) => { if (!origin) return callback(null, true); }
findings.remediation: 1. In production, reject null origins. 2. Use a whitelist that only includes known frontend domains. 3. Allow null origin only in development environment.

findings.title: Security Headers Disabled (Helmet CSP and COEP)
findings.description: Helmet is configured with crossOriginEmbedderPolicy: false and contentSecurityPolicy: false. These are critical security headers that prevent clickjacking, MIME-type sniffing, and code injection attacks.
findings.impact: The API responses lack Content-Security-Policy headers, making them vulnerable to content injection and data exfiltration if any HTML rendering occurs.
findings.evidence: main.ts line 29-30: helmet({ crossOriginEmbedderPolicy: false, contentSecurityPolicy: false })
findings.remediation: 1. Enable CSP with a restrictive policy tailored to the API. 2. Enable COEP. 3. If Swagger UI requires relaxed CSP, use a path-specific override instead of disabling globally.

findings.title: Database SSL Certificate Verification Disabled
findings.description: When DB_SSL is enabled, the PostgreSQL connection uses rejectUnauthorized: false, which disables TLS certificate verification. This makes the connection vulnerable to man-in-the-middle attacks where an attacker can intercept database traffic.
findings.impact: An attacker on the network path between the API and database can intercept all database queries, including certificate passwords, financial data, and user credentials.
findings.evidence: database.service.ts line 30-33: ssl: this.configService.get('database.ssl') === 'true' ? { rejectUnauthorized: false } : undefined
findings.remediation: 1. Set rejectUnauthorized: true in production. 2. Provide the CA certificate via DB_SSL_CA environment variable. 3. For development, keep the relaxed setting but log a warning.

findings.title: Swagger UI Always Enabled with Persistent Authorization
findings.description: Swagger UI is unconditionally mounted at /api in all environments, including production. The persistAuthorization: true option stores the JWT token in browser localStorage, which is accessible to XSS attacks. The Swagger UI also exposes the full API schema, making reconnaissance trivial.
findings.impact: Full API schema exposed in production. JWT tokens stored in localStorage are vulnerable to XSS. Attacker can use Swagger UI as an interactive attack tool.
findings.evidence: main.ts line 122-126: SwaggerModule.setup('api', app, document, { swaggerOptions: { persistAuthorization: true } });
findings.remediation: 1. Disable Swagger UI in production (NODE_ENV=production). 2. Remove persistAuthorization: true. 3. If needed in production, protect with IP whitelist or additional authentication.

findings.title: Refresh Token Not Rotated or Revoked
findings.description: The refresh token endpoint validates the token and generates new tokens, but does not revoke or rotate the old refresh token. A single refresh token can be used repeatedly for 7 days. If a refresh token is stolen, the attacker has persistent access that cannot be revoked without server-side token blacklisting.
findings.impact: Stolen refresh tokens provide persistent access for up to 7 days. No mechanism exists to revoke compromised tokens. Session hijacking is difficult to detect and remediate.
findings.evidence: auth.service.ts line 76-91: refreshToken() verifies the token and calls generateTokens() but never invalidates the old refresh token. No token blacklist or jti claim.
findings.remediation: 1. Implement refresh token rotation (issue new refresh token, invalidate old one). 2. Add jti claim to tokens and maintain a token blacklist in Redis. 3. Add a revoke-token endpoint. 4. Reduce refresh token lifetime from 7 days.

findings.title: Financial Rounding Uses JavaScript Native toFixed()
findings.description: The XmlBuilderService.formatDecimal() uses value.toFixed(decimals) for financial amount formatting, which relies on IEEE 754 floating-point arithmetic. This can produce incorrect rounding for certain decimal values. The project includes decimal.js as a dependency but does not use it in the XML builder.
findings.impact: Financial rounding errors in electronic invoices could cause tax calculation discrepancies. While each individual error is small, accumulated errors across many invoices could be significant and may cause SRI rejection.
findings.evidence: xml-builder.service.ts line 236-238: private formatDecimal(value: number, decimals: number): string { return value.toFixed(decimals); } — decimal.js is in package.json but unused here.
findings.remediation: 1. Replace toFixed() with decimal.js for all financial calculations. 2. Create a shared DecimalUtils service. 3. Ensure all monetary values use Decimal type throughout the pipeline.

findings.title: XML Parsing Libraries May Allow XXE
findings.description: The xml-signer.service.ts uses xmldom DOMParser and xml2js for XML processing. While xmldom's DOMParser in recent versions disables external entities by default, the explicit configuration to disable XXE (noent: false) is not set. The xml2js.Parser in xml-builder.service.ts also does not explicitly configure entity processing.
findings.impact: If XML input from external sources (SRI responses, uploaded XMLs) contains malicious entity declarations, it could lead to file disclosure or server-side request forgery.
findings.evidence: xml-signer.service.ts line 156-157: const parser = new DOMParser(); const xmlDoc = parser.parseFromString(xmlString, 'application/xml'); — No explicit XXE configuration. xml-builder.service.ts line 731-736: new xml2js.Parser({ explicitArray: false, ignoreAttrs: false }) — no entity config.
findings.remediation: 1. Explicitly configure DOMParser to disable external entities. 2. Set xml2js parser option noent: false explicitly. 3. Validate XML against XSD schemas before parsing. 4. Consider using a hardened XML parser.

findings.title: Static File Serving Exposes PDF and Image Directories
findings.description: ServeStaticModule serves multiple file directories (/pdfs/con_firma, /pdfs/others, /pdfs/documents, /pdfs, /images) without any access control. Any authenticated user (or unauthenticated if @Public()) can access any PDF or image file from any tenant.
findings.impact: Cross-tenant file access. A user from Tenant A could download signed invoices, PDFs, and images belonging to Tenant B by guessing or enumerating file paths.
findings.evidence: app.module.ts lines 66-91: ServeStaticModule serves /pdfs and /images directories without guards or tenant filtering.
findings.remediation: 1. Add authentication guards to static file serving. 2. Implement tenant-based file access control. 3. Use signed URLs with expiration for file downloads. 4. Move static serving behind the same JWT guard.

findings.title: SHA-1 Used in XML Digital Signatures (SRI Regulatory Constraint)
findings.description: The XML signer uses RSA-SHA1 for digital signatures and SHA-1 for reference hashing. SHA-1 is cryptographically broken with known collision attacks. However, this is mandated by the SRI Ecuador XAdES-BES standard, which requires SHA-1 for compliance. This is an accepted risk due to regulatory requirements.
findings.impact: Theoretically vulnerable to SHA-1 collision attacks, but exploitation requires nation-state resources. In practice, the SRI Ecuador infrastructure also uses SHA-1, making this a systemic limitation rather than an isolated weakness.
findings.evidence: xml-signer.service.ts line 177: name: 'RSA-SHA1', line 187: hash: 'SHA-1', line 234: hash: 'SHA-1' (importPrivateKey). All are required by SRI XAdES-BES spec.
findings.remediation: ACCEPTED RISK - SRI Ecuador Regulatory Requirement. 1. Monitor SRI for migration to SHA-256. 2. When SRI upgrades, migrate signatures to SHA-256 immediately. 3. Document this accepted risk in the security register.
